title: Mental Health — Antidepressants: Classes & Drug-Specific Effects (Set 9)
subtitle: SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, bupropion, mirtazapine, trazodone — side effects & teaching
scenario: Understanding the major antidepressant classes, their distinctive side effects, safety concerns, and client teaching.

steps.q: Which are COMMON side effects of SSRIs (e.g., sertraline, fluoxetine)?

opts.text: Nausea, sexual dysfunction, and initial jitteriness or sleep changes
opts.rat: SSRIs commonly cause GI upset (nausea), sexual dysfunction, headache, and initial anxiety/insomnia; many effects ease over time.

opts.text: A tyramine (hypertensive) reaction to cheese
opts.rat: Tyramine reactions occur with MAOIs, not SSRIs.

opts.text: Immediate complete relief of depression in one dose
opts.rat: Antidepressants have a delayed onset, not one-dose relief.

opts.text: No side effects of any kind
opts.rat: SSRIs do have side effects that should be monitored.

steps.q: To avoid antidepressant discontinuation syndrome (dizziness, flu-like symptoms), an SSRI should be:

opts.text: Stopped suddenly once the client feels better
opts.rat: Abrupt discontinuation can trigger withdrawal-like symptoms and relapse.

opts.text: Tapered gradually rather than stopped abruptly
opts.rat: Stopping an SSRI abruptly can cause discontinuation symptoms; it should be tapered under the prescriber's direction.

opts.text: Doubled and then stopped
opts.rat: This is unsafe and not how antidepressants are discontinued.

opts.text: Skipped randomly to 'wean' it
opts.rat: Erratic dosing does not safely taper the medication.

steps.q: A client on an SNRI such as venlafaxine or duloxetine should be monitored for:

opts.text: A drop in blood pressure to zero
opts.rat: This is not an SNRI effect.

opts.text: Permanent hearing loss
opts.rat: This is not associated with SNRIs.

opts.text: Increased blood pressure
opts.rat: SNRIs can raise blood pressure (dose-dependent), so blood pressure should be monitored.

opts.text: Immediate weight gain of 50 pounds
opts.rat: This is not a typical or realistic SNRI effect.

steps.q: A client taking a tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) such as amitriptyline is likely to experience which side effects?

opts.text: A tyramine reaction to aged cheese
opts.rat: Tyramine reactions occur with MAOIs, not TCAs.

opts.text: Increased salivation and diarrhea
opts.rat: TCAs tend to cause dryness and constipation, the opposite.

opts.text: No effect on the mouth or bowels
opts.rat: Anticholinergic effects on the mouth and bowels are common with TCAs.

opts.text: Anticholinergic effects — dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision, urinary retention
opts.rat: TCAs have strong anticholinergic effects (dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision, urinary retention) along with sedation and orthostatic hypotension.

steps.q: Why is extra caution needed when a suicidal client is prescribed a tricyclic antidepressant (TCA)?

opts.text: TCAs can be lethal in overdose
opts.rat: TCAs are cardiotoxic and can be fatal in overdose; prescribers often limit quantities for at-risk clients, and safety monitoring is important.

opts.text: TCAs are completely harmless in any amount
opts.rat: TCAs are dangerous in overdose, not harmless.

opts.text: TCAs cure suicidal thoughts instantly
opts.rat: No antidepressant instantly eliminates suicidal thoughts.

opts.text: TCAs cannot be swallowed
opts.rat: TCAs are oral medications; the concern is overdose lethality.

steps.q: A client just started on a TCA reports dizziness when standing up quickly. The technician recognizes this as:

opts.text: A sign the medication is not absorbed
opts.rat: Dizziness on standing reflects orthostatic hypotension, not lack of absorption.

opts.text: Orthostatic hypotension, a common TCA effect that increases fall risk
opts.rat: TCAs commonly cause orthostatic hypotension; clients should rise slowly, and fall precautions are important.

opts.text: An allergic reaction requiring epinephrine
opts.rat: This is an expected side effect, not anaphylaxis.

opts.text: Something to ignore entirely
opts.rat: It increases fall risk and should be addressed, not ignored.

steps.q: One advantage of bupropion over SSRIs is that bupropion:

opts.text: Causes a tyramine reaction
opts.rat: Tyramine reactions are an MAOI concern, not bupropion.

opts.text: Always causes heavy sedation
opts.rat: Bupropion tends to be activating, not heavily sedating.

opts.text: Is less likely to cause sexual dysfunction
opts.rat: Bupropion generally does not cause the sexual side effects common with SSRIs, which can improve adherence.

opts.text: Works instantly in one dose
opts.rat: Like other antidepressants, it has a delayed onset.

steps.q: Bupropion should be used with caution or avoided in clients who have:

opts.text: Perfectly normal health
opts.rat: These clients are not the ones for whom bupropion is cautioned.

opts.text: A desire to quit smoking
opts.rat: Bupropion is actually used to aid smoking cessation.

opts.text: Sexual side effects from an SSRI
opts.rat: Bupropion is sometimes chosen precisely because it lacks sexual side effects.

opts.text: A seizure disorder or an eating disorder
opts.rat: Bupropion lowers the seizure threshold and is generally avoided in clients with seizure disorders or eating disorders (such as bulimia/anorexia).

steps.q: Besides depression, bupropion is also used to help with:

opts.text: Smoking cessation
opts.rat: Bupropion is FDA-approved (as a specific formulation) to aid smoking cessation in addition to treating depression.

opts.text: Curing tuberculosis
opts.rat: Bupropion does not treat tuberculosis.

opts.text: Lowering cholesterol
opts.rat: It is not a lipid-lowering medication.

opts.text: Treating a broken bone
opts.rat: It has no role in fracture care.

steps.q: Mirtazapine is sometimes chosen for a depressed client who also has insomnia and poor appetite because it:

opts.text: Causes severe weight loss and insomnia
opts.rat: Mirtazapine tends to increase, not decrease, appetite and to aid sleep.

opts.text: Tends to cause sedation and increased appetite/weight
opts.rat: Mirtazapine's sedating effect and tendency to increase appetite can benefit a depressed client with insomnia and poor appetite; it is often given at bedtime.

opts.text: Has no effect on sleep or appetite
opts.rat: Its effects on sleep and appetite are precisely why it may be chosen here.

opts.text: Must be taken only in the early morning
opts.rat: Because it is sedating, it is commonly given at bedtime.

steps.q: A client taking trazodone for sleep reports a prolonged, painful erection. The technician should:

opts.text: Tell the client it is normal and ignore it
opts.rat: Priapism is a medical emergency, not a normal effect to ignore.

opts.text: Double the trazodone dose
opts.rat: Increasing the dose is unsafe and inappropriate.

opts.text: Recognize possible priapism and seek immediate medical attention
opts.rat: Priapism (a prolonged, painful erection) is a rare but serious trazodone effect that is a medical emergency requiring prompt care.

opts.text: Wait several days before mentioning it
opts.rat: Delaying care for priapism risks permanent harm.

steps.q: Which statement about antidepressants across classes is TRUE?

opts.text: They all work within a few hours
opts.rat: Antidepressants do not produce full benefit within hours.

opts.text: They should be stopped the moment mood improves
opts.rat: Stopping early risks relapse; therapy continues as prescribed.

opts.text: They are all safe to combine with any other drug
opts.rat: Some combinations (e.g., serotonergic drugs) are dangerous.

opts.text: Full therapeutic effect typically takes about 2–4 weeks or longer
opts.rat: Regardless of class, antidepressants generally take 2–4+ weeks for full effect; clients need this teaching to maintain adherence.

steps.q: The technician teaches a client to continue their antidepressant even after feeling better because:

opts.text: Continuing as prescribed helps prevent relapse
opts.rat: Antidepressants are continued for a prescribed period even after improvement to prevent relapse; stopping early is a common cause of recurrence.

opts.text: Feeling better means the depression is permanently cured
opts.rat: Improvement does not mean it is safe to stop; relapse can occur.

opts.text: The medication is addictive and cannot be stopped
opts.rat: Antidepressants are continued to prevent relapse, not because of addiction.

opts.text: It has no effect on relapse
opts.rat: Continuing therapy specifically reduces relapse risk.

steps.q: If an SSRI causes a client insomnia, a reasonable teaching point (per the prescriber) is to:

opts.text: Take a double dose at midnight
opts.rat: Doubling the dose is unsafe.

opts.text: Take the activating dose earlier in the day
opts.rat: When an antidepressant is activating and causes insomnia, taking it earlier in the day (as directed) can help; timing changes are made with the prescriber.

opts.text: Stop the medication without telling anyone
opts.rat: Clients should not stop abruptly on their own; report the problem instead.

opts.text: Drink coffee at bedtime
opts.rat: Caffeine worsens insomnia.

steps.q: A client is distressed about sexual side effects from an SSRI. The BEST advice is to:

opts.text: Immediately stop the medication without telling anyone
opts.rat: Abruptly stopping risks discontinuation symptoms and relapse.

opts.text: Ignore it forever and say nothing
opts.rat: Unaddressed side effects are a leading cause of nonadherence; they should be discussed.

opts.text: Report the side effect to the prescriber, who may adjust therapy
opts.rat: Sexual side effects should be reported to the prescriber, who may adjust the dose or switch medications; the client should not stop abruptly on their own.

opts.text: Double the dose to overcome it
opts.rat: Increasing the dose is not a safe self-directed solution.

steps.q: When an older adult is started on an antidepressant, the technician anticipates the principle of:

opts.text: Starting at the maximum dose immediately
opts.rat: High starting doses increase adverse effects in older adults.

opts.text: Ignoring blood pressure and balance
opts.rat: These must be monitored, especially in older adults.

opts.text: Avoiding all monitoring
opts.rat: Monitoring is essential, particularly early in therapy.

opts.text: 'Start low and go slow,' watching for orthostatic hypotension and falls
opts.rat: Older adults are more sensitive to medication effects; lower starting doses, slow titration, and monitoring for orthostasis/falls are prudent.

steps.q: When switching a client from an SSRI to an MAOI, a washout period is required to prevent:

opts.text: Serotonin syndrome
opts.rat: An adequate washout between an SSRI and an MAOI prevents dangerous serotonin syndrome from overlapping serotonergic effects.

opts.text: A broken bone
opts.rat: This is unrelated to the medication switch.

opts.text: Improved mood too quickly
opts.rat: This is not a safety concern requiring a washout.

opts.text: Hair growth
opts.rat: This is irrelevant to the washout.

steps.q: In addition to depression, the SNRI duloxetine is also commonly used to treat:

opts.text: Bacterial infections
opts.rat: It is not an antibiotic.

opts.text: Certain chronic pain conditions (e.g., neuropathic pain)
opts.rat: Duloxetine is used for some chronic pain conditions, including diabetic neuropathic pain and fibromyalgia, in addition to depression/anxiety.

opts.text: High cholesterol
opts.rat: It is not a lipid-lowering agent.

opts.text: A common cold
opts.rat: It does not treat viral colds.

steps.q: A client on an SSRI mentions they also take St. John's wort and occasional tramadol. The technician recognizes a risk of:

opts.text: A tyramine hypertensive crisis
opts.rat: That specific reaction relates to MAOIs and tyramine foods.

opts.text: No interaction at all
opts.rat: These combinations carry a real serotonergic interaction risk.

opts.text: Serotonin syndrome from combining serotonergic substances
opts.rat: St. John's wort and tramadol are serotonergic; combined with an SSRI they raise the risk of serotonin syndrome, so this should be reported.

opts.text: Improved safety
opts.rat: The combination increases, not decreases, risk.

steps.q: A common reason clients stop taking their antidepressant is bothersome side effects. The BEST nursing approach is to:

opts.text: Tell the client side effects are all imaginary
opts.rat: Dismissing side effects harms trust and adherence.

opts.text: Advise the client to stop the medication whenever annoyed
opts.rat: Abruptly stopping risks discontinuation symptoms and relapse.

opts.text: Ignore adherence entirely
opts.rat: Supporting adherence is a key part of care.

opts.text: Educate the client and encourage them to report side effects rather than quit abruptly
opts.rat: Teaching about expected effects and encouraging clients to report problems (instead of stopping on their own) improves adherence and safety.

reference: Aligned to the California BVNPT Psychiatric Technician exam content area: Mental Health Care. Written from standard psychiatric-technician and nursing references. Pending faculty review.
